Through improved efficiencies, management is expecting to generate $1b in incremental free cash flow each year from eFY26-28 and an additional $4b resulting from Willow coming online in eFY29.

Willow is ConocoPhillips’ Alaska North Slope project in the National Petroleum Reserve on the western side of the region. The project has an estimated 600MMbbl of recoverable reserves and is expected to produce 180Mbbl/d at its peak. The project is expected to cost $7-7.5b, with first oil coming online in eFY29. As of Q4 ’25, the project is 50% complete with 4 wells fully permitted.

Despite the strong outlay for eFY26, ConocoPhillips may face some headwinds in the Middle East with the emerging Iranian war. Accordingly, natural gas assets in Qatar have been the target of missile strikes, impacting both infrastructure and gas fields in the region. As a result, Qatar has halted LNG output and declared force majeure on LNG shipments, taking the appropriate safety precautions to protect personnel in the region. The Iranian war may create substantial setbacks for ConocoPhillips’ North Field East (NFE) and North Field South (NFS) projects. As of Q4 ’25, NFE was 80% complete and was expected to start up in e2H26. If this project was severely impacted by the war in Iran, ConocoPhillips’ eFY27-28 $1b incremental free cash flow outlay could be significantly impacted, which I suspect we’ll gain more insight on in the Q1’26 earnings release on April 30, 2026.

As for U.S. operations, ConocoPhillips has commercial LNG offtake agreements for 10.2MTPA that are expected to commence between 2026-2031, with regasification capacity in Europe of 6.7MTPA. This, in part, will benefit from the ramp-up of the Port Arthur Liquefaction facility through its partnership with Sempra, where ConocoPhillips owns 30% of the equity interest.

As part of its asset optimization efforts, ConocoPhillips is considering the sale of $2b of Permian assets in the Delaware Basin acquired through deals with Concho Resources and Shell. This would follow the $2.5b in asset dispositions in 2025, nearing management’s target of disposing of $5b in assets by the end of eFY26.

ConocoPhillips Financial Position & Forecast

eFY26 will be another year of operational improvements and cost controls with the intent of driving down operational and capital costs with the target of achieving more than $1b in a cost-reduction run-rate. ConocoPhillips is expected to improve its operational structure in eFY26, reducing operating expenses by $400mm, in part resulting from operational synergies with its 2024 Marathon Oil acquisition.

For Q1 ’26, I’m forecasting ConocoPhillips to generate $16.6b in net revenue with a diluted EPS of $1.95/share. My basis for these estimates includes the strengthened oil prices towards the end of the quarter paired with improved cost management. Looking through eFY26, I’m forecasting oil prices to remain elevated in Q2 ’26 before normalizing to the low-to-mid-$70/bbl in e2h26 in accordance with the strip price. Higher oil prices should create substantial margin expansion for ConocoPhillips for e1H26 before normalizing while leaning more heavily into management’s cost-disciplined approach for operations in the second half of the fiscal year.

ConocoPhillips closed FY25 with $6.9b in cash & equivalents and $23.4b in debt on the balance sheet for a net leverage ratio of 0.65x net debt/aEBITDA. In FY25, ConocoPhillips paid down $900mm in debt, reducing its net debt position by $1.75b to $16.5b when compared to the close of FY24.

Risks Related to ConocoPhillips

Bull Case

ConocoPhillips is positioning its assets to focus more heavily on gas export, an appealing market as the war in Iran disrupts the international LNG cargo supply chain. While ConocoPhillips’ Qatar assets may be negatively impacted by the Iranian war, I believe the firm has capacity outside of the region that can partially offset these losses, especially as commodity prices remain elevated. Additional cost synergies post-Marathon acquisition as well as cost discipline should drive margins for the coming years.

Bear Case

ConocoPhillips’ Qatar assets may be negatively impacted by the war in Iran, potentially leading to a substantial impact on the planned cash generation for eFY27-28.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

ConocoPhillips returned 45% of operating cash flow to shareholders in FY25 in the form of share repurchases and dividend distributions, and is expecting to maintain this target for eFY26. This translates to $5b in shares repurchased in FY25 on top of the $4b returned through its dividend distribution. As of the close of FY25, ConocoPhillips had $25.7b remaining under its existing share repurchase authorization.

Overall, I believe ConocoPhillips is well positioned in the market as the organization reshapes its assets to focus on high-yielding production, particularly as it develops its Alaska assets. With the potential for increasing demand for U.S.-borne LNG cargoes, I believe ConocoPhillips will realize an appealing uplift in earnings potential over the coming years. Fundamentally, I believe COP shares should be priced at $160/share at 5.25x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA; I am recommending COP shares with a Buy rating.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.
